summary: Gruber highlights USA Today columnist Kevin Maney's experience with a devastating Windows virus that left his PC in an endless reboot loop, and the wave of 'get a Mac' reader mail that followed. Maney acknowledged that Macs and Linux machines 'hardly get infected or invaded at all,' though he hedged with 'for whatever reasons.' Gruber seizes on that phrase as the crux of the issue: regardless of why Windows is less secure, the practical reality is that Windows users are plagued by malware while everyone else is not. He notes that despite Microsoft's years of security promises, new viruses were up 400% year over year. Gruber argues that ordinary, non-technical users are beginning to realize the cycle of patches and anti-virus software will never end, and the best solution is to stop using Windows altogether.
key_points: Kevin Maney's personal Windows malware nightmare illustrates the typical home user experience with Windows security failures
The reasons why Windows is more vulnerable than alternatives are debatable, but the practical outcome is not — Windows users suffer disproportionately from malware
Despite years of Microsoft's security improvement promises, the situation was getting measurably worse, with new viruses up 400%
Non-technical users are reaching a tipping point where they recognize the patch-and-protect cycle is futile
The best solution to the Windows security problem, for ordinary users, is simply not to use Windows
money_quotes: "For whatever reasons" is the key point I tried to make in "Broken Windows" back in June. The reasons why are subject to argument. But you can't argue about the net effect: Windows users, especially with their home computers, are plagued by insidious malware; everyone else is not.

New Windows updates, new anti-virus software, new ad-blocking software — regular people are starting to realize that the cycle never ends, that they are never going to successfully secure Windows, and that the easiest and best solution to the Windows security problem is not to use Windows at all.

Kevin Maney is not a nerd. Enthusiast, maybe, but not a nerd. What's interesting about these two columns isn't technical insight into the nature of the problem; he has none. But the vast majority of Windows users aren't nerds, either, and Maney seems to have his finger on their pulse.
Microsoft has been talking about improved security in Windows for the last few years. But the situation continues to get worse.
key_insight: The debate over why Windows is less secure than alternatives is beside the point — what matters is the undeniable reality that Windows users are drowning in malware, and ordinary people are finally realizing the only real fix is to stop using Windows.
